title: Investor Overview PDF
subtitle: AI checkpoint infrastructure for regulated workflows.
shortDescription: Market timing, product path, commercial route and expansion logic for Corentis.
evidenceHook: Frames Corentis at the intersection of regulated-service pressure and rapid AI adoption.
ctaText: Request an investor or design partner conversation.
disclaimer: This document is for discussion and pilot exploration only. It does not constitute legal, regulatory, investment or compliance advice.

sections.heading: Opening position
sections.body: Corentis Shield is an AI checkpoint for regulated workflows. It is designed to sit between AI agents and regulated actions, checking proposed outputs against policy, risk rules, human-review requirements and evidence requirements before anything sensitive moves forward.

sections.heading: The core problem
sections.body: AI adoption is accelerating, but regulated teams cannot rely on AI agents in sensitive workflows unless actions are controlled, reviewed and evidenced. The risk is not only that AI may produce a weak answer. The risk grows when that answer becomes a customer message, record update, recommendation or workflow action.

sections.heading: Why now: AI adoption is moving faster than operational control
sections.body: Corentis sits at the intersection of two trends: rising regulated-service pressure and rapid AI/agentic AI adoption. The opportunity is not simply to add more AI, but to make AI actions controlled, reviewable and evidenced.

sections.heading: Why policies alone are insufficient
sections.body: Policies describe intent, but they do not automatically control runtime AI behaviour. Corentis is designed to create a checkpoint before action, so proposed outputs can be checked, routed, paused or recorded before they affect customers, teams or live systems.

sections.heading: The Corentis answer
bullets: Corentis Shield: the checkpoint before AI outputs become real-world action.
Policy Controls: operational rules derived from policy, risk and evidence requirements.
Review Queue: human approval and escalation where judgement is needed.
Assurance Lab: scenario testing before live use.
Evidence Vault: records of proposals, decisions, reviews and timestamps.
Pilot Report: a structured evidence pack for rollout decisions.

sections.heading: Narrow wedge, broader platform
sections.body: The first wedge is financial-services complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows because they combine high contact volume, sensitive customer context, evidence requirements, escalation rules and reputational risk. Once the control pattern works for one regulated workflow, it can be adapted to other regulated support, casework and AI-agent workflows.

sections.heading: Commercial value
bullets: Faster safe AI rollout through a clear checkpoint-before-action pattern.
Clearer operational oversight around AI-assisted work.
Reusable evidence instead of manual evidence chasing.
Reduced duplicated governance effort across pilot workflows.
Stronger procurement and buyer confidence through evidence-led controls.

sections.heading: What Corentis is seeking
bullets: Design partners with sensitive regulated workflows.
Pilot conversations around complaints, vulnerability and regulated support.
Strategic investors and funders who understand AI assurance infrastructure.
Regulated workflow collaborators who want a practical path from review to deployment.

title: Assurance & Governance Summary PDF
subtitle: How Corentis supports controlled AI workflows and evidence-led assurance.
shortDescription: How Corentis maps AI outputs to controls, review and evidence.
evidenceHook: Shows why assurance needs evidence captured while the workflow runs.
ctaText: Review the sample pilot report or discuss an assurance workflow.
disclaimer: Corentis supports governance and assurance workflows. It does not replace legal, compliance, risk or regulatory judgement. This document is for discussion and pilot exploration only.

sections.heading: What Corentis is
sections.body: Corentis Shield is a checkpoint and evidence layer for AI-assisted regulated workflows. It is designed to help teams review sensitive outputs, route exceptions and capture evidence before action.

sections.heading: What it helps control
bullets: Human approval requirements before sensitive action.

sections.heading: Why assurance needs runtime evidence
sections.body: The commercial risk is not only that an AI model may be wrong. It is that a firm may be unable to show which controls operated, why a sensitive action was paused, who reviewed it, and what evidence was captured.

sections.heading: Control loop
bullets: Check AI outputs or actions at runtime.

title: Design Partner Pack PDF
subtitle: A practical path to pilot Corentis Shield on one regulated workflow.
shortDescription: A practical outline for starting with one sensitive workflow.
evidenceHook: Explains why complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows are a strong first pilot wedge.
disclaimer: This document is for discussion and pilot exploration only. A design partner pilot can begin as non-production testing and should be scoped with appropriate risk, compliance and operational stakeholders.

sections.heading: Who this is for
sections.body: This pack is for regulated teams exploring AI-assisted workflows where actions need controls, review and evidence. The first pilot can be narrow, practical and non-production.

sections.heading: Why start with complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows?
sections.body: A non-production pilot can test whether AI-assisted workflows can be made more controlled before deployment: sensitive outputs paused, vulnerability signals escalated, human approval routed, and evidence captured.

sections.heading: What a pilot could test
bullets: Can policies be translated into operational controls?
Can risky AI outputs or actions be caught before action?
Can human review be routed cleanly?
Can the organisation see a clearer go/no-go path?

sections.heading: What Corentis would need
bullets: Feedback on usability and evidence needs.

title: Sample Pilot Report PDF
subtitle: Financial services complaints workflow. Demo / illustrative report.
shortDescription: A report structure for evidence-led controlled AI workflow pilots.
evidenceHook: Shows how evidence could be presented for a fictional vulnerable-customer complaints workflow.
disclaimer: This is fictional demo material. It does not describe a real customer, a real bank, a real complaint or a completed deployment. It is for discussion and pilot exploration only.

sections.heading: Executive summary
sections.body: This illustrative report shows how Corentis Shield could support a controlled pilot for a financial-services complaints assistant focused on vulnerable-customer handling. AI can assist with triage, drafting and recommendations. Sensitive customer messages and final decisions require checkpointing and human review.

sections.heading: Why this sample workflow matters
sections.body: Complaints, hardship and vulnerable-customer workflows combine customer emotion, operational pressure, evidence requirements and reputational risk. They are a practical first place to test whether AI-assisted work can be made more controlled before deployment.

sections.heading: Use-case boundary
bullets: AI can assist with triage, drafting and recommendations.
AI cannot directly send sensitive customer messages without human review.
AI cannot make final complaint decisions without evidence checks.
Hardship, vulnerability and complaint-risk signals should trigger review or escalation.

sections.heading: Control objectives
bullets: Require human approval for sensitive communications.
Capture evidence for decisions and review.

sections.heading: Scenario tests
bullets: Customer discloses job loss and missed mortgage payment: checkpoint escalates hardship signal and requires human review.
Complaint includes repeated contact after vulnerability disclosure: checkpoint blocks direct closure and asks for evidence review.
AI drafts overconfident compensation wording: checkpoint routes wording to approval and records version.
AI suggests closing a complaint without adequate investigation: checkpoint blocks closure until investigation evidence is attached.
Customer requests urgent support: checkpoint escalates to a specialist queue and records the reason.

sections.heading: Control matrix summary
bullets: Risk: vulnerability signal. Control: pause direct response. Evidence: disclosure and review note.
Risk: unsupported closure. Control: block closure. Evidence: investigation history required.
Risk: sensitive compensation wording. Control: review required. Evidence: approved wording/version recorded.

sections.heading: Go/no-go recommendation
sections.body: Proceed to a controlled design-partner pilot. Do not treat this illustrative report as approval for enterprise rollout.

title: Control Matrix Example PDF
subtitle: Illustrative example only.
shortDescription: Example control mapping for policy, risk, review and evidence.
evidenceHook: Maps vulnerable-customer and financial-difficulty pressure into reviewable controls.
ctaText: Discuss how this matrix could map to your workflow.
disclaimer: Illustrative example only. This document does not constitute legal, regulatory or compliance advice.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Handle vulnerability signals carefully and avoid inappropriate pressure.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Pause direct response when vulnerability is detected.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Human review required before customer communication.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Disclosure, proposed response, policy version and review decision.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Escalate hardship signals and consider tailored support.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Route hardship cases to specialist review.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Specialist approval before collections or closure action.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Hardship signal, missed-payment context and support review note.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Do not close complaints without adequate investigation evidence.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Block closure if investigation evidence is missing.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Complaint history, missing evidence flag and closure decision.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Ensure compensation or refund wording is accurate and approved.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Review required before sensitive wording is sent.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Draft, approved wording/version and reviewer timestamp.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Present information in a way customers can understand.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Plain-language review for complex or sensitive messages.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Review required if communication is unclear or too technical.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Original draft, revised wording and review decision.

sections.heading: Example control areas grounded in real regulatory pressure
sections.body: The example matrix shows how policy intent can become operational checks, approval points and evidence logs. It is not a template for legal compliance; it is a practical way to make review points visible in a pilot.

title: Runtime Checkpoint Explainer PDF
subtitle: A plain-English guide to the checkpoint before action.
shortDescription: A plain-English explanation of the checkpoint before action.
evidenceHook: Explains why checkpoints matter as AI agents move from drafting to acting.
disclaimer: This document is for discussion and pilot exploration only. Corentis Shield is designed to support controlled AI workflows; it does not solve every AI governance issue.

sections.heading: What a runtime checkpoint is
sections.body: A runtime checkpoint is the moment where Corentis checks what an AI system is about to say or do before that action moves forward.

sections.heading: Why checkpoints matter as AI agents move from drafting to acting
sections.body: A checkpoint is the practical control point between an AI-generated recommendation and a sensitive real-world action.

sections.heading: The difference between documents, outputs and checkpoints
bullets: A policy document describes expected behaviour.
An AI model output is a draft reply, recommendation or proposed action.
A runtime checkpoint checks the output before action.
Human review adds judgement where risk, context or evidence requires it.
An evidence log records what was proposed, checked, decided and reviewed.

sections.heading: Simple flow
sections.body: AI proposes output/action -> Corentis checks it -> safe items continue -> risky items pause -> human review -> evidence logged.

sections.heading: Examples
bullets: AI detects a vulnerable customer signal.
AI tries to close a case without enough evidence.

sections.heading: Why this matters
bullets: Less blind trust in AI outputs.
